---
title: '2484555'
author: "Bertuğ Orman"
date: "2026-05-13"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

```{r}
# ==============================================================================
# AIN-2002: DATA CLEANING, EDA, AND REGRESSION MODELING
# 2nd Homework Assignment - Spring 2026
# ==============================================================================

# --- PART 0: DATASET CREATION ---
# We initialize the dataset using the provided seed and parameters[cite: 22, 33].
set.seed(42) 

df_assignment <- data.frame(
  Student_ID = 1:50, 
  Study_Hours = c(10, 12, NA, 15, 8, 20, 22, 5, 30, NA, 14, 18, 45, 11, 9, 21, 25, 13, 16, 28, 
                  10, 12, 14, NA, 8, 19, 23, 6, 31, 15, 14, 18, 12, 11, 9, 22, 26, 13, 17, 29, 
                  10, 13, 15, 11, 7, 20, 24, 5, 32, 16), 
  Attendance_Rate = c(0.85, 0.90, 0.70, NA, 0.60, 0.95, 0.88, 0.40, 1.0, 0.75, 0.82, NA, 1.20, 0.88, 0.65, 
                      0.92, 0.98, 0.76, 0.84, 0.99, 0.85, 0.91, 0.83, 0.68, 0.62, 0.96, 0.89, 0.42, 1.0, 0.77, 
                      0.81, 0.87, 0.84, 0.89, 0.66, 0.93, 0.97, 0.75, 0.85, 0.98, 0.86, 0.92, 0.82, 0.88, 0.61, 
                      0.94, 0.90, 0.41, 1.0, 0.78),
  Extracurricular = c("Yes", "no", "YES", "No", "no", "Yes", "No", "no", "Yes", "YES", "No", "Yes", "Yes", "No", "no", 
                      "Yes", "No", "no", "Yes", "Yes", "No", "No", "Yes", "no", "No", "Yes", "No", "no", "Yes", "No", 
                      "No", "Yes", "No", "No", "no", "Yes", "No", "no", "Yes", "Yes", "No", "No", "Yes", "No", "No", 
                      "Yes", "No", "no", "Yes", "No"), 
  Final_Grade = c(75, 82, 60, 88, 55, 92, 85, 40, 98, 70, 78, 84, 250, 76, 62, 90, 95, 72, 80, 96, 
                  74, 83, 77, 65, 58, 91, 87, 42, 99, 71, 79, 85, 73, 75, 63, 92, 94, 70, 81, 97, 
                  76, 84, 78, 74, 56, 93, 88, 41, 100, 72) 
)
```

```{r}
# --- TASK 1: DATA CLEANING ---

# 1.1 Missing Values: Apply median imputation. 
# Interpretation: Median is preferred over mean because it is robust against outliers present 
# in Study_Hours and Final_Grade.
df_assignment$Study_Hours[is.na(df_assignment$Study_Hours)] <- median(df_assignment$Study_Hours, na.rm = TRUE)
df_assignment$Attendance_Rate[is.na(df_assignment$Attendance_Rate)] <- median(df_assignment$Attendance_Rate, na.rm = TRUE) 

# 1.2 Inconsistency: Normalize Extracurricular labels and convert to factor
# Interpretation: Normalization ensures that "YES", "Yes", and "no" with spaces are 
# treated as identical categories.
df_assignment$Extracurricular <- trimws(tolower(df_assignment$Extracurricular)) 
df_assignment$Extracurricular <- as.factor(df_assignment$Extracurricular) 

# 1.3 Outliers: Correct logically impossible values.
# Interpretation: Attendance cannot exceed 1.0 (100%), and grades cannot exceed. 
# We use capping (Winsorization) to correct these values.
df_assignment$Attendance_Rate[df_assignment$Attendance_Rate > 1.0] <- 1.0 
df_assignment$Final_Grade[df_assignment$Final_Grade > 100] <- 100 
```

```{r}
# --- TASK 2: EXPLORATORY DATA ANALYSIS (EDA) ---

# Visualizing distribution of Final_Grade.
hist(df_assignment$Final_Grade, 
     main = "Distribution of Final Grades", 
     xlab = "Final Grade", col = "skyblue", border = "white") 

# Comparing grades by extracurricular activity.
boxplot(Final_Grade ~ Extracurricular, data = df_assignment,
        main = "Final Grade by Extracurricular Participation",
        xlab = "Extracurricular Activity", ylab = "Final Grade",
        col = c("orange", "lightgreen")) 
```
```{r}
# --- TASK 3: CORRELATION ANALYSIS ---

# Calculate Pearson and Spearman coefficients.
pearson_corr <- cor(df_assignment$Study_Hours, df_assignment$Final_Grade, method = "pearson") 
spearman_corr <- cor(df_assignment$Study_Hours, df_assignment$Final_Grade, method = "spearman") 

print(paste("Pearson Correlation:", round(pearson_corr, 4))) 
print(paste("Spearman Correlation:", round(spearman_corr, 4))) 
# Interpretation: Spearman captures monotonic relationships, while Pearson captures linear ones. 
# A difference between them indicates potential non-linearity or influence of outliers.
```

```{r}
# --- TASK 4: REGRESSION MODELING ---

# Build multiple linear regression model.
model <- lm(Final_Grade ~ Study_Hours + Attendance_Rate + Extracurricular, data = df_assignment) 
summary(model) 
# Interpretation: Review the summary to identify variables with p-values < 0.05
```
```{r}
# --- TASK 5: MODEL PERFORMANCE AND RMSE ---

# Manual RMSE calculation.
y_predicted <- fitted.values(model) 
y_observed <- df_assignment$Final_Grade 
rmse <- sqrt(mean((y_observed - y_predicted)^2)) 
print(paste("Manual RMSE:", round(rmse, 4))) 
```

```{r}
# --- TASK 6: INTERPRETATION ---

# R-squared explanation[cite: 61].
# Interpretation: R-squared represents the proportion of variance in Final_Grade 
# explained by the independent variables in the model.

# Regression coefficient for Study_Hours.
study_coef <- coef(model)["Study_Hours"]
print(paste("Grade change per study hour:", round(study_coef, 4))) 
# Interpretation: For every one-unit increase in Study Hours, the Final Grade 
# is predicted to change by the value of this coefficient.
```

```{r}
# --- TASK 7: REGRESSION VISUALIZATION ---

# Scatter plot with regression line.
plot(df_assignment$Study_Hours, df_assignment$Final_Grade,
     main = "Study Hours vs Final Grade",
     xlab = "Study Hours", ylab = "Final Grade", pch = 19, col = "darkblue") 
abline(lm(Final_Grade ~ Study_Hours, data = df_assignment), col = "red", lwd = 2)
# Interpretation: The plot shows how closely the data points cluster around the linear fit, 
# indicating the strength of the relationship.
```

```{r}
# --- TASK 8: RESIDUAL ANALYSIS ---

# Residuals vs Fitted plot.
res <- residuals(model) 
plot(y_predicted, res, 
     main = "Residuals vs Fitted Plot",
     xlab = "Fitted Values", ylab = "Residuals", pch = 19) 
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2) 
# Interpretation: Random distribution around zero suggests linearity and homoscedasticity. 
# A funnel or U-shape would suggest violations of these assumptions.
```

```{r}
# --- BONUS: HIGH LEVERAGE POINTS ---

# Cook's Distance visualization.
cooks_d <- cooks.distance(model) 
plot(cooks_d, type = "h", main = "Cook's Distance Visualization", 
     xlab = "Index", ylab = "Cook's Distance", col = "darkred") 
# Interpretation: High Cook's Distance values identify influential observations 
# that significantly impact the regression slope.
```
